text: Hello friends, this patient has large intramural myoma that is approximately eighteen centimeter intramural myoma and we will perform the laparoscopic myomectomy. So let us see how to perform and this is the port position will be according to baseball diamond concept.

The surgeon introduces a varus needle and trocar through Palmer's point due to the patient's previous caesarean section. An epigastric port is placed, followed by adhesiolysis to separate visible adhesions caused by the previous surgery.

The surgeon has completed the removal of omental adhesions, revealing the left adnexal structures including the round ligament, fallopian tube, and ovary, which appear normal. The inspection now shifts to the right side.

The surgeon injects vasopressin into the myoma, initiating hydro dissection. The injection causes visible blabbing and swelling around the myoma, creating a clear dissection plane and inducing ischemia to minimize bleeding. The solution used consists of five international units of vasopressin mixed with twenty milliliters of saline.

After injecting vasopressin, the surgeon removes the aspiration needle and uses a harmonic scalpel to make an oblique incision through the muscle and serous layer. This incision avoids disturbing the fallopian tube and facilitates easier suturing. The zero muscular layer is carefully cut to expose the myoma.

The surgeon enlarges the incision to accommodate the approximately eighteen centimeter intramural myoma, ensuring a good dissection plane is achieved. The muscle layer is cut, revealing the capsule of the myoma.

The surgeon employs a pushing and pulling technique to enucleate the myoma, using a harmonic scalpel initially. The myoma screw is periodically repositioned to maintain effective traction. Once a good plane is achieved, the surgeon switches to a self-manufactured stainless steel myoma rod, which is blunt and atraumatic, providing strong force for enucleation without bending.

The surgeon relocates the myoma screw to the base of the myoma, ensuring proper traction for enucleation. The dissection resumes, and the myoma is stripped away smoothly due to the well-established plane.

The surgeon relocates the myoma screw and cuts the remaining superficial muscle layer over the myoma to achieve a better plane for extraction. As the dissection reaches the base, the surgeon carefully uses energy devices to manage potential bleeding from larger vessels, considering the use of bipolar or ligature if necessary.

The surgeon has nearly reached the base of the myoma, with 95% of it already separated. The remaining 5% is being carefully detached using a harmonic scalpel. Below the myoma, a small bowel is visible, requiring caution to avoid thermal injury from the hot tip of the harmonic scalpel. The final detachment is completed, fully separating the myoma.

The surgeon begins suturing by introducing the needle through the lower see dust layer into the muscle layer. Intramural muscle layers are sutured to obliterate dead space and control bleeding, with muscle-to-muscle suturing being performed.

The surgeon removes the needle from the upper end of the serous layer and begins shear-to-shear suturing. The left hand provides traction to hold and tighten the suture, ensuring proper tension while taking bites.

here we have taken all the continuous suturing but there are many options you can use barb suture you can use baseball suturing if you will search then you will find out our many videos with the baseball suture of myomectomy and then in some of the cases we have used the barb suture some cases we have used the extracorporeal square knot and extracorporeal Western knot so depending upon the situation and how much bleeding is there and how much you have the traction in between the tissue you can use a various type of the knots but here there was no bleeding and a good plane myoma is removed so we have done all continuous suturing that is a fast method of closure and it is effective also
The surgeon uses suction irrigation to remove clots from the muscle tissue, ensuring a clear surgical field. Suturing is then resumed to close the muscle layers.
Suturing need patience and ambidextrity. Your both the hand has to coordinate and keep on tightening it time to time.

The surgeon reaches the opposite end of the suturing line, where the needle is taken out through healthy tissue to properly terminate the knot. A clot attached to the tail end is removed, and the tail end, which is slightly larger, is used as the knotting end. The needle is cut and removed to facilitate knot tightening and termination.

The surgeon removes the needle and uses the tail end to create a reverse C knot with two wraps, tightening it securely. A second knot is formed with a C shape and two wraps, fed into the Maryland bag. After tightening, a final reverse C knot is made with alternating wraps. The suture is cut and removed, followed by the initiation of morselation using a storage morselator.
The surgeon begins the process of morselation, consistently pulling the tissue to create large chips. The procedure is time-consuming and will continue for an extended period.
